the longest season on the church calendar, and much like summer itself, it has a languid<|8.98|> <|8.98|> quality.<|9.78|> <|10.78|> In this season after Pentecost, we slow down a little bit.<|16.54|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> With him on this journey are James and John, the sons of Zebedee, some of the first disciples called by Jesus.<|11.82|> <|12.90|> In Mark, they are hand of Jesus in his glory.<|12.06|> <|13.54|> Perhaps it should come as no surprise then that their view of discipleship is to enact a scorched earth policy<|22.98|> <|22.98|> when Jesus is summarily <|13.18|> James and John's rhetoric is not all that dissimilar from much of what we hear today.<|20.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Many in 2019 have this same scorched earth and bury my father.'<|12.62|> <|12.62|> But Jesus said to him, "'Let the dead bury their own dead.<|16.82|> <|17.54|> But as for you, go and proclaim the kingdom.'"<|20.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And another said, "'I will follow you, Lord, but first let me say farewell to those at my home.'<|10.72|> <|10.72|> And Jesus said to him cryptically,
